segments.s267-g145: سپاه و سپهبد are the coordinated subjects ('the army and the spahbed'). In classical Persian the construction infinitive + گرفت (برفتن گرفت) is idiomatic: the infinitive (برفتن 'to depart') before گرفت yields a periphrastic past meaning 'began to depart' or 'took to departing.' گرفت here functions as an auxiliary-like verb rather than its literal 'seize' sense.
segments.s267-g146: زمین is the subject and سم اسبان is a genitive/attributive group 'the horses' hooves' (no ezāfe is shown, as is common in older/poetic Persian). نهفتن گرفت repeats the same idiom (infinitive 'to hide' + گرفت) meaning 'began to hide' or 'began to be concealed' with زمین as agent.
segments.s267-g147: تو گفتی که is a reporting clause ('you said that'). خورشید تابان is the topical noun phrase 'the shining sun'; به جای is a fixed locative phrase 'in its place' (به + جای = 'in/at its place'). The verb of the reported clause follows in the next segment.
segments.s267-g148: بماند is a third-person singular present/subjunctive used in reported speech after گفتی to express 'would/remain' or 'should remain.' از introduces the cause or source ('because of' / 'from') and نهیب سواران is a genitive phrase 'the horsemen's thunderous cry.' به پای is an adverbial locution ('at the foot/at their feet' or 'to a halt' in poetic usage) that modifies the state of remaining.
segments.s267-g149: دو هفته is a temporal phrase 'for two weeks' placed before the verb for emphasis. همی رفت is an iterative/continuous past sense ('kept going' / 'continued to go'); ز آن سان سپاه is an adverbial/qualifying phrase 'the army in that manner' (ز آن سان = 'in that way'), with سپاه as the logical subject.
segments.s267-g150: بشد is a narrative past of شدن meaning 'it happened / came to be'; here it introduces the resultant state: روشنایی 'brightness/light' (the grammatical subject) followed by ز خورشید و ماه 'from the sun and the moon' (ز marks the source). The verb precedes the subject for poetic inversion.
segments.s267-g151: پراگنده is an adjective/adverb 'widespread' placed before the locative phrase. بر گرد کشور is a prepositional phrase 'around the country' (بر گرد = 'around/about'; کشور = 'country'), and خبر is the head noun 'news' or 'report' — together forming 'widespread news around the country.'
segments.s267-g152: ز here marks source or content ('of'/'from'): ز جنبیدن شاه پیروزگر = 'of the movement/stirring of the triumphant king.' جنبیدن is the infinitive 'to move/stir' used nominally, شاه is the possessed noun 'king', and پیروزگر is an epithet 'triumphant/victor' modifying شاه.
segments.s267-g225: This short clause means ‘news reached the elders.’ به marks the indirect object (dative: ‘to/for’), پیران is the plural definite noun ‘elders,’ and رسید is a past verb used impersonally/reciprocally here (‘reached/was received’). آگهی ز این سخن is a genitive/adjunct phrase ‘news from this speech’ that identifies the content of what reached them.
segments.s267-g226: که introduces the content clause (‘that …’). سالار ایران is the noun phrase subject ‘the commander/chief of Iran,’ with the head noun سالار followed by the place-name modifier ایران. چه is an interrogative/indefinite (here functioning as ‘what’/‘how’/‘what sort of’) and افگند is the simple past (3rd sg.) ‘cast/threw’ (verb-final tendency is preserved). بن is the object meaning ‘root/foundation’ — together the clause states what the commander cast down.
segments.s267-g227: از آن آگهی is a prepositional phrase ‘from/at that news.’ شد is the past of شدن used as a copula/auxiliary ‘became,’ and دلش is the possessed noun ‘his heart’ (دل + enclitic ش = his). پرنهیب is an adjectival/nominal predicate meaning ‘full of dread/terrible’ — the whole clause = ‘his heart became filled with dread because of that news.’
segments.s267-g228: سوی چاره برگشت is ‘he turned toward a remedy/solution’: سوی marks direction, چاره is the goal ‘remedy,’ and برگشت is the past verb ‘returned/turned’ (i.e. resorted). The coordinating و introduces بند و فریب (‘fetters/bonds and deception’), a coordinated nominal phrase that names the means or devices he resorted to; syntactically these nouns complete the picture of what he turned to.
segments.s267-g229: ز دستور فرخنده رای is an ablative/agentive phrase ‘by/on the order of the auspicious counsel’: ز = ‘by/from,’ دستور = ‘order/command,’ modified by فرخنده رای (‘auspicious counsel/fortunate plan’). آنگهی is an adverb ‘then/thereupon’ placed after the phrase to signal the subsequent action.
segments.s267-g230: بجست is the past tense verb ‘he sought’ (sought out, pursued). اندر آن introduces the locative/mental frame ‘in/within that,’ followed by the compound noun phrase کینه جستن رهی — کینه جستن is a verbal-noun ‘seeking hatred/vengeance’ and رهی (here functioning as ‘a way/path’) names what he sought. Together: ‘he sought, within that grudge-seeking, a way’ i.e. a way to pursue vengeance.
segments.s267-g129: چو ... شد is a temporal clause meaning "when ... happened." ز + پیش (از پیش) here means "from (the) presence of/away from before," so the phrase reads literally 'when from before Khosrow went out Hejir.' Note the proper name هجیر stands after the verb شد in this poetic order (verb final element plus name).
segments.s267-g130: سپهبد is the agent/title (the general). همی is the classical continuous/intensive particle marking iterative or ongoing action. رای زد is an idiom where رای (counsel/plan) is combined with the light verb زد (literally 'strike') to mean 'offer/voice a plan' or 'deliberate'; با وزیر states the co-actor/partner ('with the vizier'). Word order (subject – adverbial particle – object+light-verb – complement) is typical of older Persian.
segments.s267-g131: ز بس + noun is a poetic way to say 'from so much / because of so great' (ز = 'from/because of', بس = 'so much' or 'enough') and مهربانی is the noun 'kindness.' The relative/linker که introduces the result or explanation clause that follows.
segments.s267-g132: سراسر همه رزم is an adverbial phrase meaning 'throughout all the battle(s)/campaign(s).' بد رای شاه is a noun phrase 'the king's bad/ill counsel' where بد (adjective) precedes رای (counsel/plan) and شاه is the possessor; classical Persian often stacks nouns without an explicit ezāfe in poetry.
segments.s267-g133: همی marks repeated or continuous speech ('kept saying'). اگر introduces a conditional ('if'), followed by the conditioned noun phrase لشکر افراسیاب ('Afrasiab's army'). The verb of the conditional appears in the next segment.
segments.s267-g134: بجنباند is a causative/present-stem verb meaning 'set in motion / cause to move' (here conditional: 'should move'). از جای = 'from its place/position.' و connects clauses. بگذارد آب literally 'let/allow the water' (i.e. 'let the water pass' or 'let the river run'), where بگذارد is causative/allowing; this is left deliberately open and the clause continues the consequence of the condition.
segments.s267-g135: سپاه مرا places the possessor after the possessed in poetic word order: 'my army.' را is the object/topic marker attached to the possessed phrase. بگسلاند is a transitive perfective/cause verb meaning 'would break apart / disperse.' ز جای is an adverbial 'from its place/position.'
segments.s267-g136: مرا رفت باید is an idiomatic ordering meaning 'it is necessary that I go' (باید + infinitive/verb expresses obligation; here the personal element 'مرا' marks the person obliged). همینست = 'this is exactly so / that is it,' and رای closes the sentence: 'this is the counsel/decision.' Note the compact, slightly elliptical poetic order (the clauses are stacked rather than fully fleshed out).
segments.s267-g185: سپهدار is the subject (the military commander). The preposition بر marks the target of the praise (literally 'toward/on the king'), so the phrase means 'the commander to/at the king'. کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made' used with the noun آفرین as its object, so کرد آفرین = 'expressed praise' or 'praised'. Word order is somewhat inverted/poetic but follows classical Persian use of کرد + noun.
segments.s267-g186: به فرمان is a prepositional phrase 'by/at the command (of [him])' with به given the instrumental/causal sense 'by/at'. The verb ببوسید is a past narrative form meaning '(he) kissed' (subject is the same actor carried over from the previous clause). روی زمین is a locative phrase 'on/upon the ground' (روی = 'on/upon', زمین = 'ground/earth').
segments.s267-g187: ببود is an archaic/poetic past copula meaning 'it was' (classical variant of بود), so ببود آن شب = 'it was that night'. رای زد is a compound verb meaning 'took counsel' or 'considered/decided' (رای = counsel/plan; زد is the verbal element forming the idiom). با پسر marks the companion 'with his son'. The overall clause is a past narrative describing a deliberation.
segments.s267-g188: به شبگیر بنشست uses بنشست (past 'sat down') with به + شبگیر indicating association/position ('with the night-keeper' or 'at the night-watch'); this is a somewhat compressed classical phrasing. The conjunction و links the clauses, and بگشاد در is a past verb with the prefix ب- (بگشاد = 'he opened') followed by در meaning 'door' (here 'he opened the door'). The implicit subject continues from the previous clause.
segments.s267-g189: همه نامداران لشگر پگاه groups modifiers before the verb: همه 'all', نامداران 'the renowned/illustrious ones' modifying لشگر 'army', and پگاه 'dawn' gives the time ('at dawn' or 'the dawn's ...'). This is the nominal subject phrase meaning 'all the famous men of the army at dawn'.
segments.s267-g190: برفتند is the past plural 'they departed/they went forth'. The following phrase is a participial/locative sequence: بر سر نهاده کلاه literally places the locative first in poetic order — 'having placed (نهاده, past participle) a helmet (کلاه) on (بر) the head (سر)'. Thus the clause means they set out with helmets upon their heads.
segments.s267-g191: پس is a temporal/consecutive particle 'then'. آن نامه شاه is the noun phrase 'that letter of the king' (the genitive relationship is indicated by juxtaposition: نامه شاه = 'the king's letter'). فرخ هجیر is the personal name (here the agent who will bring the letter); the comma separates the topicalized object/phrase from the subject in poetic word order.
segments.s267-g192: بیاورد is a past narrative verb form 'he brought' (classical narrative style). The conjunction و links the next action, and بنهاد is another past narrative verb from نهادن 'to place/put' (here 'placed'). پیش دبیر is a locative phrase 'before/in the presence of the scribe' (پیش = before/in front of, دبیر = scribe/secretary), indicating where the letter was placed.
segments.s267-g161: This is a simple clause: proper name (هجیر) + past-tense verb آمد ('came') followed by a prepositional phrase از پیش + possessor + noun. از پیش here means 'from before / in front of', and خسرو دمان is a genitive juxtaposition ('Khosrow's hem'), with دمان a poetic word for the hem or skirt of a robe.
segments.s267-g162: A string of adjectival/adverbial phrases joined by و describing the manner and state of the subject: گرازان here gives a boar-like manner ('grunting/snorting like boars'), خندان is a present-participle adjective 'smiling/laughing', and دل شادمان is a nominal phrase 'heart (دل) glad/joyful'. These modify هجیر in the previous clause.
segments.s267-g163: ابا here functions as an archaic/poetic form of با ('with') introducing an accompanying list: خلعت 'robe (of honour)', خوبی 'beauty/goodness', و خرمی 'and delight/mirth'. In older texts the vowel/orthography may differ; the whole phrase is an instrumental/with-phrase.
segments.s267-g164: تو گفتی is the main clause 'you said'. همی is an archaic particle (here an intensifier/modality) appearing before the verb; برنوردد is a third-person present/subjunctive-form meaning 'would/shall overrun, accomplish, traverse'. زمی (زَمی) is adjectival/nominal meaning 'of the earth' or 'earthly' and is the object of برنوردد: 'overrun the earth.' Note: the lexical entry for گفتی in the supplied senses does not list the verbal sense 'you said', so it is treated separately.
segments.s267-g165: چو آمد is a temporal clause ('when he came'); چو is the classical conjunction 'when', آمد is past tense 'came'. به نزدیک + پرده‌سرای is a prepositional phrase 'near the stage/theatre' (pرده‌سرا = stage/scene). Word order is typical: temporal subordinate clause followed by main clause.
segments.s267-g166: برآمد 'arose/issued forth' is the main verb and is followed by the verbal noun/infinitive خروشیدن + the instrument/agent کرنای; the sequence means 'the roaring (خروشیدن) of the trumpets (کرنای) rose up.' The verb-first order is common in poetry for emphasis.
segments.s267-g167: پذیره شدندش is a compound where پذیره (reception/acceptance) with شدند (they became) plus the enclitic object -ش ('him') yields 'they received him' or 'they accepted him.' سران 'the chiefs' is the subject; سر به سر is an idiomatic repeat meaning 'one and all / each in turn.' Word order places the verb before the subject for poetic effect.
segments.s267-g168: This is a parallel adjectival construction: [زمین] پر ز [آهن] = 'the ground full of iron' and [هوا] پر ز [زر] = 'the air full of gold.' پر ز is the fixed pattern 'full of' (پر + ز meaning 'full from/of'), with poetic nouns آهن (iron/—here poetically 'weapons') and زر (gold).
segments.s267-g137: همانگه is an adverbial phrase meaning 'at that moment / immediately' placed at the front for emphasis. شه is the subject 'the king' (classical spelling). نوذران را is the definite object with را marking the people being summoned; را here is the object marker. بخواند is an older/narrative past 3rd-person form meaning 'he called/summoned' (classical usage rather than modern simple past).
segments.s267-g138: بفرمود is an honorific past verb 'he commanded/ordered'. تا introduces a purpose/result clause here ('so that / in order that'). تیز modifies لشکر and means 'swift' (describing the host). براند is the 3rd-person form of راندن used here in a jussive/purpose clause after تا: 'that (they) drive/put (the host) in motion' — i.e. 'make the host advance.'
segments.s267-g139: به سوی is a two-part prepositional phrase 'toward/into' indicating direction. دهستان is the place 'district / village-territory' that is the goal. سپه is the noun 'host / army' acting as the moving entity, and برکشید is a classical verb meaning 'raised / drew / moved' — here used poetically of an army advancing toward a place.
segments.s267-g140: همه دشتِ خوارزم is the subject phrase 'the whole plain of Khwarezm' (همه + noun + genitive). لشکر کشید is the predicate: classical usage of کشیدن with لشکر means 'mustering/bringing forth an army' or 'sent forth an army'; the verb is used here in a transitive/figurative sense ('mustere[d] / drew out an army').
segments.s267-g141: نگهبان لشکر بود is a nominal predicate 'he was the guard/sentinel of the army' with بود as the past copula. روز جنگ is a temporal adverbial 'on the day of battle' placed after the copula to specify when/under what condition he served as guard.
segments.s267-g142: به جنگ اندر آید is a standard classical expression meaning 'he would go into battle / he entered into the fray'; اندر is the old preposition 'in/into' and آید here is a narrative/habitual present used to describe his customary action. به سان پلنگ is a simile 'like a panther/leopard' (به سان = 'like/as'). The clause therefore describes his manner of entering battle.
segments.s267-g143: تبیره برآمد uses برآمد (past 'arose/issued forth') with ز (poetic form of از 'from') to say that a signal/sound came out of a place. درگاه طوس is a genitive 'the court / presence of Tūs' (درگاه = royal presence/court; طوس = the hero Tūs). تبیره is an archaic/poetic noun referring to a signal or blast here (the instrument/signal that rose).
segments.s267-g144: خروشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive 'the roaring/crying out' and here completes the previous clause by specifying what rose. نای رویین is a tight poetic compound: نای 'the reed/ney (flute)' qualified by رویین 'iron-hard / sounding like iron' (a poetic epithet), و connects items, and کوس is 'the war-drum'. Together the phrase describes the loud blowing/beating of flute and drum.
segments.s267-g97: Structure: an affected-person phrase + verb + object + adverbial phrase. 'به ما' marks who is affected (classical usage here = ‘from/against us’). 'برکند' is a third-person present/jussive/periphrastic verb meaning 'seize/take away' (archaic diction); it governs the following noun 'پیشدستی' (the thing taken). 'به جنگ' is a prepositional phrase 'in/for battle' that specifies the domain of that action.
segments.s267-g98: 'نبیند کس' is a negative jussive/optative construction: the negative verb-form (نبیند) precedes 'کس', giving the sense 'may no one see' / 'let no one behold'. The remaining phrase 'این روز تاریک و تنگ' is a noun phrase (demonstrative + head noun + two adjectives) meaning 'this dark and narrow/cramped day' and is the object of 'see'.
segments.s267-g99: 'بفرمایم' is a conventional first-person hortative/performative (literally ‘I command / I say’) used to introduce what follows; it is followed by the temporal adverb 'اکنون' ('now') and the subordinating particle 'که' ('that'). 'بر پیل کوس' is a prepositional/locative phrase 'on/against the elephant, a drum' (here: ‘a drum upon an elephant’), where 'بر' = 'on/upon' and 'کوس' = 'war-drum'.
segments.s267-g100: The clause introduced by 'که' has the verb 'ببندد' (3rd-person jussive/present: 'let him bind' / 'may he fasten'). 'دمنده' (literally 'the blower' or trumpeter/drummer) is the actor, and 'سپهدار طوس' is an appositive/title phrase identifying him as 'the commander Tūs' (in classical Persian apposition often follows the noun). Grammatically the verb precedes its subject here, a common poetic/order-flexible structure.
segments.s267-g101: This is a piled list of places/territories joined by 'و' (and). 'دهستان و گرگان و آن بوم و بر' is an idiomatic pairing: 'بوم و بر' = 'land and territory' (a fixed collocation). The whole phrase is the object/group of objects that the coming verb will act upon.
segments.s267-g102: Two verbs in sequence describe successive actions. 'بگیرد' (3rd-person present/optative) = 'he seizes/takes' and governs the preceeding list of places. 'برآرد' (archaic literary form) = 'he brings up / raises' and here takes the prepositional phrase 'به خورشید سر' ('his head toward the sun') — the object 'سر' (head) follows the goal 'به خورشید' in a poetic order ('raises his head to the sun').
segments.s267-g103: Standard nominal prepositional cluster: 'من' (I) + 'اندر پی طوس' = 'in pursuit/of Tūs' (i.e. 'following Tūs'). 'با پیل' = 'with an elephant' (instrumental/companional), and 'و گاه' = 'and at times/occasionally' (adverbial). Word order is flexible here because of poetic compression.
segments.s267-g104: 'به یاری بیایم' is a verbal phrase with the dative preposition 'به' + noun 'یاری' (help) + present/jussive-like first-person verb 'بیایم' (here future/intentional: 'I will come / I come to help'). 'به پشت سپاه' is a locative phrase 'to the rear of the army' (پشت = rear; سپاه = army) specifying where the help will be rendered.
segments.s267-g65: This is an introductory nominal fragment: 'و' = and; 'دیگر' = 'others' or 'the rest'; 'که' here is the relative/connector 'who/that'; 'از لشکران' is a prepositional phrase 'from/of the armies' with the preposition 'از' before the plural noun 'لشکران'; 'گران' is a postpositive adjective meaning 'heavy/weighty' describing the armies. The whole chunk sets up a relative idea ('and others who, from the heavy armies...') and is left syntactically open to be completed by the next segment.
segments.s267-g66: 'پراگنده' is a participial/adjectival predicate 'scattered' that completes the idea begun in the previous fragment ('others ... were scattered'). 'بر گرد' is a prepositional phrase meaning 'around/about' (here 'بر' functions with 'گرد' to mark surrounding); note the inverted order 'توران سران' (possessor before head) is a poetic ordering meaning 'the chiefs of Turan.'
segments.s267-g67: 'بدو' is the prepositional pronoun 'to him/unto it' placed before the noun phrase; 'دشمن آمد' is a simple past-clause 'the enemy came'; 'ز هر سو پدید' is an adverbial modifier 'appearing/from every side' with 'ز' (from) + 'هر سو' (every side) and the participle/verb 'پدید' (appeared) completing the picture. Word order is typical classical Persian: indirect object/prepositional pronoun + subject + past verb + adverbial phrase.
segments.s267-g68: 'از آن' = 'from that/there' (آن = demonstrative). 'بر لب رود جیحون' is a locative phrase 'onto the bank (لب) of the river Jeyhun' where 'بر' = toward/to, 'لب' = brink/bank, and 'رود جیحون' = 'the Jeyhun River' (routinely phrased as 'رود جیحون' in classical Persian). 'کشید' is the past tense verb 'drew/moved/withdrew' completing the clause; here it denotes motion toward the river bank.
segments.s267-g69: 'به پنجم سخن' = 'as to the fifth speech/point' where 'به' introduces the topic and 'پنجم سخن' specifies the ordinal topic. The particle 'که' here introduces a conditional/subordinate clause 'that/if/when', and 'آگهی خواستی' is past-tense second-person 'you sought/wanted information' (خواستی = you wanted), so the segment reads 'as for the fifth point, when/if you wanted information'.
segments.s267-g70: The verb phrase 'دل بیاراستی' (دل = heart as direct object before the verb, بیاراستی = archaic past/subjunctive form 'you adorned/you should adorn') has 'به مهر گوان' as an adverbial/prepositional modifier 'with/unto love like so' ('مهر' = love/affection, 'گوان' = 'like/so'). In other words: 'adorn the heart with such love.' Note that 'بیاراستی' is an older verbal form (second-person) used in Ferdowsi's diction.
segments.s267-g71: This is a series of similes introduced by the comparative 'چو' ('like/as'). 'چو لهراسب' = 'like Lohrasp'; 'و چون اشکش' = 'and like Ashkash'; 'تیزچنگ' follows as an epithet—here functioning as a name/epithet attached to Ashkash (thus treated as a proper name, Tizchang). The repetition of 'چو/چون' is a poetic device listing famed figures for comparison.
segments.s267-g72: Continues the comparative series: 'چو رستم سپهبد' = 'like Rostam the spahbed' (سپهبد is an appositive title after the name). 'دمنده نهنگ' is a nominal phrase 'the whale that blows' where 'دمنده' is a participle/adjective 'breathing/blowing' modifying 'نهنگ' (whale); together it serves as a metaphoric epithet in the same series of comparisons. Word order is name + title, then adjective + noun as epithet.
segments.s267-g169: چو is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when.” خیزد is a third-person singular present (classical) form of خاست/خیز meaning “rises/ascends”; in this clause the verb comes before its noun phrase (typical of Ferdowsi's diction). به چرخ اندرون is a prepositional phrase “into the inner sky/heavens,” and داوری is the thing that “rises” (the subject/nominal that the verb refers to): “when judgment rises into the inner heaven.”
segments.s267-g170: ز ... و ز ... (here poetic ز) means “from.” The phrase lists celestial sources: ز ماه و ز ناهید وز مشتری = “from the Moon and from Nahid and from Jupiter.” وز before مشتری is the contracted sequence و + ز (and + from) in poetic spelling.
segments.s267-g171: بیاراست is a past tense/preterite third‑person singular meaning “he arrayed/arrayed well/arrayed (for battle).” لشکر is the direct object “the army/host.” چو + چشم خروس is a temporal simile “at (or like) the cock’s eye,” i.e. at cockcrow/dawn.
segments.s267-g172: ابا in the manuscript reading is best understood here as a poetic/archaic form of آوا (sound) meaning “the sound of,” so the phrase lists martial sounds and implements: “(the) sound of golden bells and elephants and war‑drums.” زنگ زرین = “golden bells,” پیلان = “elephants,” کوس = “war‑drum.”
segments.s267-g173: چو آمد is “when he came.” بر here means “to/toward” (direction: he came to someone). نامور پهلوان = “the renowned/illustrious champion” (adjective + noun); note the verb precedes the prepositional phrase in this classical word order.
segments.s267-g174: بگفت is the reporting verb “he said/relayed.” آنچ (= آن‌چــه) = “that which/what.” دید is the past tense ‘saw’ (what he had witnessed). از here means “concerning/of” and introduces the topic: از شه خسروان = “concerning the king(s)/the royal ones.”
segments.s267-g175: نوازیدن here is an infinitive/verbal noun meaning “to praise, flatter, or court (favor)” (poetic use). شاه = “the king.” و = “and.” پیوند اوی = “his bond/alliance/connection” (پیوند as ‘link, bond’; اوی as possessive “his/her”). The infinitive phrase functions as a noun phrase describing the action performed.
segments.s267-g176: همی is an adverb meaning “continually/still/ever.” گفت is the verb “he said/spoke” (here used as a repeated action: he kept saying). از introduces the topic “about/concerning”; رادی = “generous, magnanimous”; و = “and”; پند = “advice/counsel”; اوی = possessive “his.” So: “he continually spoke about his generosity and his counsel.”
segments.s267-g201: به marks the indirect object (dative) 'to'; روزی‌دهان is a compound noun 'giver of livelihood' functioning as the recipient; داد is a past-tense transitive verb 'gave/handed'; یکسر is an adverb modifying the giving, 'entirely/wholly/at once'; کلید is the direct object 'the key'. Word order is somewhat compressed (recipient before verb), typical of classical Persian.
segments.s267-g202: چو here = 'when' (temporal conjunction) and آمد is the past 'came' forming 'when came / when it came to pass'; گه is the noun 'occasion/time' (object of چو); نام جستن is an infinitival phrase 'to seek a name' where نام = 'fame/reputation' precedes the verb جستن 'to seek'; پدید is the verb 'appeared/manifested' (inverted order from the more usual پدید آمد).
segments.s267-g203: برافشاند is a past verb 'he sent forth / spread / threw out' (a forceful, distributive verb). بر + لشکر gives the target 'against/upon the army'; آن qualifies لشکر as 'that/specified' host; خواسته is a participial/adjectival form 'desired/summoned' modifying لشکر (i.e. 'the force that had been asked for').
segments.s267-g204: سوار و پیاده are coordinated nouns 'horse (men) and foot (soldiers)'; شد is the past auxiliary/verb 'became' or 'were made'; آراسته is a past-participle/adjective 'adorned/arrayed', so شد آراسته together indicate a resultant state: 'were made/arrayed (ready)'.
segments.s267-g205: یکی is an indefinite 'a certain / one'; لشکری is 'an army / troops' (here 'an army'); گشن is an adjective 'hungry/ravenous' qualifying لشکری; بر سان is a classical simile 'like/as' (بر سان = 'like'), followed by کوه 'mountain' as the term of comparison. The order places the simile after the described noun.
segments.s267-g206: زمین is the subject 'the land'; از پی is a compound indicating cause/sequence 'from / because of / in the wake of' (از + پی); بادپایان is a plural/collective 'the fleet-footed / swift ones' (the pursuers); ستوه is a predicate adjective 'weary / exhausted', so the clause means 'the land was weary from the pursuit of the fleet-footed.'
segments.s267-g207: دل (heart) + شیر (of the lion) forms a genitive/attributive phrase 'the lion's heart' or 'the heart of a lion'; غران is a participial adjective 'roaring' modifying شیر (or the compound دل شیر); از ایشان indicates cause 'because of them'; به بیم is an idiomatic phrase 'with fear' (به used adverbially here) and the copula 'was' is implied in classical style.
segments.s267-g208: همه 'all/everyone' is the subject; غرقه is an adjective/participial predicate 'drenched/immersed'; در introduces the medium 'in/within' followed by a triad آهن و زر و سیم (iron, gold, and silver) — a poetic listing of arms and riches (آهن here often stands for weapons/steel).
segments.s267-g153: چو is a temporal conjunction meaning 'when' here. طوس is the proper name Tūs. از + در form the prepositional phrase 'from the court/gate' (در used as 'court/gate' here) and شاه ایران is a genitive phrase 'the king of Iran'; برفت is the archaic past form meaning 'he went/left' (past of رفتن).
segments.s267-g154: سبک here means 'light' (as in light-armed or swift) used substantively/adjectivally to describe the kind of force; شاه is 'of the king' or 'kingly' as a modifier in context; رفتن is the infinitive 'to go' used within the clause; بسیچید is a past-form meaning 'he mustered/gathered' (from بسیجیدن) and is the main verb of this fragment; تفت is unclear or archaic in this line (does not fit the provided senses) and likely an aberrant or intensifying word.
segments.s267-g155: ابا is a poetic/spelling variant of با 'with' here (introducing accompaniment). ده هزار is the numeral phrase 'ten thousand'; از marks a subset 'of/from'; گزیده is an adjective 'chosen/selected' modifying سران 'heads/chiefs/leaders'.
segments.s267-g156: همه 'all' quantifies the following nouns. نامداران 'the famed/renowned ones' is a plural noun; و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'; کنداوران is a plural noun here meaning a class of fighters/seasoned men in this context (the provided modern glosses don't match exactly, so treat it as a group of veteran/combatant figures).
segments.s267-g157: به نزدیک is a directional phrase 'toward/near'; گودرز is the proper name Goudarz; بنهاد is the past of نهادن 'he placed/put' and here forms the idiom بنهاد روی 'to set/turn one's face (toward)' ; روی 'face' is the object of بنهاد in that idiom (i.e. 'he turned his face toward Goudarz').
segments.s267-g158: ابا again functions as with (poetic spelling of با) indicating accompaniment; نامداران 'the renowned ones' names the companions; پرخاشجوی is an adjective 'quarrelsome/aggressive' modifying نامداران.
segments.s267-g159: ابا/با 'with' introduces a list of martial accoutrements. پیل is 'elephant' (used literally or figuratively for great war-elephants); و is 'and'; با again 'with'; کوس is a war-drum; و is 'and'; با introduces the following item; فرهی here is adjectival/nominal and in context names a kind of noble or noble display (the exact classical sense varies), functioning as part of the list of splendours/accoutrements.
segments.s267-g160: ابا (با) 'with' continues the listing; تخت is 'throne'; و 'and'; با 'with' again; تاج is 'crown'; شاهنشهی is a compound adjective/noun meaning 'imperial' or 'of kingship', modifying تاج (i.e. 'the imperial crown').
segments.s267-g81: "وز آن‌سو که لهراسب شد با سپاه" is a locative clause starting with وز (a poetic/compound preposition 'from/and on' — here used to mark 'on that side'), followed by آن‌سو 'that side' and the relative particle که 'where/that'. لهراسب شد is a past-state clause: شد is used as a past copula/auxiliary meaning 'was/was situated', and با سپاه is an accompaniment phrase 'with an army' placed after the verb.
segments.s267-g82: "همه مهتران برگشادند راه" has subject (همه مهتران 'all the chiefs/commanders') followed by the verb برگشادند 'they opened/cleared' and the object/goal راه 'the road/way'. برگشادند is third-person plural past (they opened); همه is distributive 'all/everyone'; مهتران is the plural agent noun 'chiefs/commanders' (classical/formal word), placed before the verb as is normal in Persian narrative.
segments.s267-g83: "الانان و غز گشت پرداخته" lists two peoples/parties (الانان 'the Alans' and غز) joined by و, followed by گشت پرداخته: گشت is a verbal/root meaning 'turned/was made' (here marking change/state), and پرداخته is a past participle form meaning 'dealt with/subdued/treated' — together indicating those peoples were brought under control.
segments.s267-g84: "شد آن پادشاهی همه ساخته" continues with شد as the auxiliary 'was' (forming a passive or resultative), آن پادشاهی 'that kingship/kingdom' as the subject/theme, and همه ساخته 'entirely made/established' (همه modifying ساخته). The word order places the auxiliary before the noun-phrase for poetic emphasis; ساخته is a past participle meaning 'made/established'.
segments.s267-g85: "گر افراسیاب اندر آید به راه" is a conditional clause introduced by گر 'if'. افراسیاب is the subject. اندر 'in/onto' + آید 'comes' form the verb phrase 'comes in/onto'; به راه is a directional/locative phrase 'on the road' or 'along the way'. The verb آید is present/future-oriented here under the conditional.
segments.s267-g86: "ز جیحون بدین سو گذارد سپاه" is the result clause: ز جیحون 'from the Jihon (Amu Darya)' marks the river-side, بدین سو is a deictic directional phrase 'to this side / on this side' (here split in the segmentation: ب-دین-سو), گذارد is a third-person singular past/future-marked verb 'places/sets' (classical narrative uses this to mean 'will set/position'), and سپاه 'army' is the object placed. Word order is verb after the locatives, typical in Persian narrative.
segments.s267-g87: "بگیرند گردان پس پشت اوی" has the verb بگیرند (third-person plural subjunctive/optative or future-oriented narrative use) 'they seize/take', followed by گردان 'troop/unit' as the object. پس پشت اوی is a collocation meaning 'his rear/back' (پس 'after' + پشت 'back'), with اوی as the possessive 'his'. The phrase indicates taking his troops from both front/back or seizing his rear-guard.
segments.s267-g88: "نماند به جز باد در مشت اوی" is a resultative clause: نماند 'remains/is left' (negated here by context: 'nothing remains'), به جز 'except', باد 'wind' (literal), در مشت 'in the fist/hand', اوی 'his'. The construction expresses the idiom 'nothing remains for him except wind in his fist' (i.e., he is left with nothing tangible).
segments.s267-g33: کنون is a temporal adverb 'now'. چون here introduces a temporal/conditional clause ('when/as'); نبیند is a third-person present/subjunctive form of دید ‘to see’ and, together with جز + NP, expresses 'sees none except X' (the جز phrase follows the verb to mean 'except'). Word order is adverb → subordinating particle → verb → exception phrase.
segments.s267-g34: دلش را is a definite direct object: دل + possessive suffix ش 'his' and the object marker را. تو is the understood addressee 'you' (subject of the following verb). از مهر او is an ablative/causal phrase 'because of / from his love' (از + noun + pronoun). برمتاب is the finite verb addressing the addressee (imperative/optative/hortative poetic form) meaning roughly 'do not yield/turn over' or 'do not give' — it takes دلش را as its object. The segment therefore directs the listener: do not give his heart away because of his love.
segments.s267-g35: گر is the protasis particle 'if'. او is the third-person subject 'he'. بر before خرد means 'over/against/in place of' (he chooses هوا instead of خرد). برگزیند is a present/subjunctive/optative form of 'to choose' and governs the phrase; هوا is the thing chosen ('desire, whim'). The clause reads 'If he chooses desire over wisdom.'
segments.s267-g36: به کوشش is an adverbial phrase 'by/with effort'. نروید is a verbal form meaning 'will not grow/sprout' (ن- prefix + روید from روییدن); here it negates the growth. ز خارا گیا is an ablative/genitive phrase 'from flinty rock' (ز = 'from/of', خارا = flint/rock, گیا = vegetation/plant). The clause says that even with effort, vegetation will not sprout from hard flint.
segments.s267-g37: تو is the addressee 'you'. با دشمن 'with/to the enemy' marks the interlocutor. ار is the conditional 'if'. خوب here modifies گفتن/gویی to mean 'well/kindly'. گویی in syntax is a verb 'you speak' (subjunctive/present) but the provided sense list does not include this verbal sense, so it is treated as the main verb of the protasis. رواست is the predicate 'it is permissible/fitting' (روا + copula ست). The clause states the conditional permissibility of kind speech to an enemy.
segments.s267-g38: از marks the target/concern 'of/regarding'; آزادگان is the plural 'the free (men)'. خوب گفتن is a deverbal phrase 'speaking well/praising' (خوب modifies the verbal noun گفتن). سزاست is an impersonal/fixed predicate 'it is deserved/fit'. The whole nominal clause 'از آزادگان خوب گفتن' functions as the subject/complement of سزاست: 'to speak well of the free is fitting.'
segments.s267-g39: و دیگر is a connective 'and besides/furthermore'. ز here is the poetic genitive/partitive 'concerning/about' (z + noun) and introduces the domain 'پیکار جنگ‌آوران' — 'the battle/fighting of the warriors' (پیکار modified by the plural noun جنگ‌آوران). This phrase sets the topical sphere for the following question.
segments.s267-g40: کجا is the interrogative 'where'. یاد کردن here functions as 'to remember, to mention, to bring to mind'; کردی is the second-person past 'you did' (you mentioned/remembered). به + NP (به گرز گران) marks the thing referred to — 'regarding the heavy mace(s)' (گرز = mace, گران = heavy). The phrase asks 'Where did you mention (speak of) the heavy mace in the warriors' fighting?' Note the adjective follows the noun (گران after گرز), a normal Persian order.
segments.s267-g9: خجسته سپهدار بسیار هوش is a compact nominal phrase describing a person. خجسته is an adjectival epithet placed before the title سپهدار (a military title, 'commander'); بسیار is an adverbial intensifier modifying هوش ('intellect, wisdom'). The whole segment functions as the first item in a poetic series of attributes.
segments.s267-g10: This is a parallel coordinate phrase built with repeated همه to mean completeness. همه رای و دانش reads 'complete in counsel and knowledge' (همه qualifying the following nouns), and then همه جنگ و جوش repeats the pattern to mean 'complete in war and ardor.' The conjunction و simply links the paired nouns.
segments.s267-g11: A nominal coordination: خداوند functions as an honorific/title ('lord') followed by the name گوپال; و links that name to تیغ بنفش ('the purple sword'). In the poem these nouns are juxtaposed without additional inflection, forming a terse list of exalted items or epithets.
segments.s267-g12: فروزنده is a participial/adjectival form meaning 'radiant/illuminating' and qualifies the noun phrase کاویانی درفش ('Kayanian banner'). Note the poetic ordering: فروزنده precedes the adjectival qualifier کاویانی and noun درفش, producing 'the radiant Kayanian banner.'
segments.s267-g13: سپاس از introduces the beneficiary or object of thanks ('thanks to / praise for'). جهاندار یزدان ما is a noun phrase where جهاندار ('world-ruler' / sovereign) and یزدان ('God') form a title, with ما ('our') marking possession. The preposition از marks the entity to whom thanks are directed.
segments.s267-g14: که introduces a relative clause ('who/that'). پیروز بودند is a past-tense verbal phrase ('were victorious'), with بودند (past of بودن) agreeing in number; گردان ما ('our troop') is the logical subject placed after the verb in this poetic order. The clause therefore states that our troop were victorious (by the grace/power of the sovereign).
segments.s267-g15: از اختر ('from a star') marks the source; ترا is the object form of 'you' written as a 2nd-person enclitic (ت) plus the object marker را, so ت+را = 'you' as direct object. روشنایی is the noun 'brightness/light', and نمود is the past tense of نمودن used here as 'caused/made/appeared' — together 'he made brightness for you from a star.'
segments.s267-g16: ز (poetic form of از) marks source 'from the enemy.' برآورد is used as a past verb meaning 'brought forth/raised' (classical usage), ناگاه is an adverb 'suddenly', and دود is the noun 'smoke'. The clause thus reads 'suddenly smoke rose/brought forth from the enemy' — a compact V‑S order typical in poetic diction.
segments.s267-g1: دبیر خردمند را is the definite direct object: noun (دبیر) followed by a postpositive adjective (خردمند) and the object marker را. پیش خواند is a compound where پیش (here 'in the presence of / before') combines with the past verb خواند (3sg) meaning 'he called/summoned'; together they mean 'he summoned (into his presence)'.
segments.s267-g2: سخنهای بایسته is a noun phrase 'the fitting/necessary words' with بایسته as a postpositive adjective. با او is a prepositional phrase 'with him'. براند is a poetic/older use of راندن (3sg) here functioning to mean 'put forth / addressed / dealt out' (i.e. he directed those words to him). The whole segment says he addressed appropriate words to that man.
segments.s267-g3: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. آن نامه را is the definite object 'that letter' marked by را. زود is an adverb 'quickly/soon' modifying the verb; پاسخ نوشت is a noun + verb combination 'wrote an answer' (نوشت is 3sg past). Together: 'when he quickly wrote a reply to that letter.'
segments.s267-g4: پدید آورید is an archaic/poetic past form of آوردن (3sg) meaning 'made appear / disclosed'. اندر او = 'in it' (او refers back to the letter/reply). خوب و زشت are the two nouns/adjectives 'the good and the bad' which are what he caused to appear in the text. Syntactically the verb (آورید) governs the phrase اندر او خوب و زشت: 'he manifested in it the good and the bad.'
segments.s267-g5: نخست 'first / at first' sets temporal order. آفرین کرد is a conventional collocation (آفرین + کرد) meaning 'praised' or 'gave praise' (کرد is 3sg past auxiliary). بر کردگار uses بر with the recipient: 'to/unto the Creator' (کردگار = God).
segments.s267-g6: کز is a fused relative/prepositional form meaning 'from whom' here (که + از). او refers back to the Creator/God. دید is 3sg past 'saw / beheld / experienced'. نیک و بد روزگار is the noun phrase 'the good and the bad of روزگار', where روزگار functions as 'fate / the course of time'—so the clause explains why he praised the Creator (because from Him he had seen good and ill of fate).
segments.s267-g7: دگر here means 'again / then / next' indicating another act. آفرین کرد again is 'praised' (آفرین + کرد, past 3sg). بر پهلوان = 'to the hero/champion' (prepositional phrase marking recipient).
segments.s267-g8: که is the relative pronoun 'who'. The sequence جاوید بادی و روشن‌روان are predicative adjectives applied to the hero: جاوید 'eternal', بادی literally 'wind‑borne / like the wind' (a poetic epithet, i.e. swift or airy), and روشن‌روان a compound adjective 'bright‑souled / light‑spirited'. The copula (بود) is omitted as is common in poetry.
segments.s267-g193: This is a noun phrase composed of a subject and its modifiers: 'دبیر' (the scribe/secretary) is specified by the temporal phrase 'آن زمان' (of that time). Following that is the object phrase 'پند و فرمان شاه' where the possessed noun (پند و فرمان — 'advice and commands') takes its possessor after it ('شاه' = 'the king'), the normal Persian possessed–possessor order.
segments.s267-g194: This contains the verbal clause. 'ز + نامه' is a prepositional phrase 'from the letter'. 'همی' is the archaic/prosodic marker that makes the past verb imperfective or habitual (classical Persian progressive), and it attaches to 'خواند' (past tense 3rd sg.) meaning 'read/recited'. 'پیش سپاه' is a locative/prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of the army'.
segments.s267-g195: 'سپهدار' is the subject 'the commander'. 'روزی‌دهان را' is the definite object (marked by 'را') — 'the provider' or 'the one who gives sustenance'. The verb 'بخواند' here means 'called/summoned' (classical usage where the verb conveys summoning) in the past third-person form.
segments.s267-g196: 'بدیوان' is an adverbial/locative phrase 'into/at the court'. 'دینار دادن' is a verbal-noun construction literally 'giving dinars' (purpose or complement), with 'دادن' as the infinitive/gerund 'to give'. 'نشاند' is a past transitive verb meaning 'set/caused to sit' (archaic past of 'to seat'), so the clause reads 'he seated (him) in the court to give dinars.'
segments.s267-g197: The segment begins with the preposition 'ز' ('from/of') marking origin. 'اسبان گله' is a genitive-like compound 'herds of horses' and 'هرچ بودش' is an existential clause 'whatever there was' where 'بودش' is past tense 'was' with enclitic pronoun '-ش' ('it/there'). 'به کوه' gives the location 'on the mountain' (به used with place).
segments.s267-g198: 'به لشکرگه' is the goal/place phrase 'to the army-camp'. The verb 'آورد' is past third-singular 'brought'. 'یکسر گروه' is the object: 'یکسر' is an adverbial/quantifying word meaning 'entirely/at once' modifying 'گروه' ('group, company, troop'), so syntactically 'he brought the whole troop at once to the camp.'
segments.s267-g199: 'در گنج' is a locative prepositional phrase 'in the treasury'. What follows is a coordinated list of items ('دینار و تیغ و کمر') joined by 'و' — 'dinar (coins) and sword(s) and girdles/belts'. There is no overt copular verb; classical Persian often omits 'was' in such inventory phrases.
segments.s267-g200: 'همان' here is a demonstrative/intensifier ('that very'/'the same') modifying the following phrase. 'مایه‌ور جوشن' is a noun phrase 'a wealthy/rich cuirass' (مایه‌ور as 'wealthy/rich in [ornament]'). The 'و' links it to 'خود زر' where 'خود' works as an intensifier/reflexive 'the very/gold itself' and 'زر' is 'gold'. Together they continue the list of treasures inside the treasury.
segments.s267-g209: بفرمودشان is past tense ‘he commanded/ordered (them)’ with the object-clitic -شان. جنگ را ساختن is an infinitival complement (جنگ marked by را as a definite/accusative object) meaning ‘to make/prepare war’; the whole phrase is the thing commanded.
segments.s267-g210: This is a coordinate infinitival phrase governed by the same command: دل و گوش دادن is ‘to give (one's) heart and ear’ (two nouns sharing the verb دادن), و links the two ideas, and به کین آختن is an infinitive phrase ‘to (be) set on / to take up vengeance’ with به marking its object کین; آختن is an archaic/poetic infinitive meaning roughly ‘to be kindled/engaged (in)’. All three infinitives are complements of بفرمودشان.
segments.s267-g211: برفتند is past plural ‘they went/advanced’. پیش here means ‘before / in the presence of’, so the prepositional phrase precedes the subject phrase سپهبد گروه; in poetic word order the verb appears before its subject, and سپهبد گروه (“the spahbed of the troop” / ‘the troop’s commander’) identifies whom they approached.
segments.s267-g212: بر governs the following noun phrase as ‘against / facing’; انبوه لشکر is a dense/numerous host, and به کردار کوه is a simile introduced by به indicating manner (‘in the manner/form of a mountain’). Together they describe the troop confronting the packed army like a mountain.
segments.s267-g213: بر ایشان is ‘upon/at them’; نگه کرد is a light-verb construction (noun نگه ‘gaze’ + کرد ‘did’) meaning ‘he gazed/looked’. سالار مرد follows the verb in the poetic order and functions as the subject (‘the chief/leader, the man of valor’).
segments.s267-g214: This is an elliptical, poetic sight report: زمین تیره دید = ‘he saw the earth dark’ (تیره modifies زمین; دید = ‘saw’), and the following آسمان لاژورد supplies a parallel image ‘the sky (was) lapis-azure’ without a copula. Adjectives follow the nouns and the verb can follow its objects in this poetic order.
segments.s267-g215: چنین گفت is a terse, poetic 'thus said' construction: چنین (an adverb 'thus/so') precedes گفت (the verb 'said'), with the speaker omitted (third person). کز is a contracted classical conjunction (که + از) meaning 'that/from which' and here introduces a temporal clause 'since'. گاه رزم پشین = 'the time of (the) former battle' where گاه 'time' is modified by the genitival noun رزم 'battle' and the adjective پشین 'former' follows the noun in older/poetic order.
segments.s267-g216: نیاراست here functions as an older/poetic negative/perfect verbal form of آراستن ('to make/prepare/arrange'), with the negative prefix نـ; it means 'has not made/has not fitted' (the exact aspect is past/perfect). The inverted word order places the verb before کس 'no one' (common in Persian poetic style), and رزمگاهی چنین is the object phrase 'a battlefield such as this' (رزمگاهی 'battle-site' + postpositive demonstrative/qualifier چنین). The clause thus says that nobody has ever prepared a battlefield like this since that earlier time.
segments.s267-g105: تو (subject) comes first as the addressed ‘you’. از جنگ پیران is a prepositional phrase ('away from / from the battle of the elders') placed before the verb for emphasis. مبرتاب is a negative imperative/prohibitive form (poetic) meaning 'do not turn/withdraw', and روی (lit. 'face') is the thing being turned away (object), so the whole clause reads 'You, do not turn your face away from the elders' battle.'
segments.s267-g106: سپه را uses را as the definite/object marker ('the army' as the object). بیارای is an imperative verb here meaning 'array/put in order / make ready' (classical/poetic). The conjunction و links to the next phrase; ز (poetic form of از) introduces the target/agent 'از او' contracted as زو ('from him'), and کینه‌جوی is a predicative adjective/participial description 'seeking vengeance' — i.e. 'array the army and seek vengeance on him.'
segments.s267-g107: چو is a temporal/conditional conjunction 'when'. هومان و نستیهن are coordinated proper names (subject/participants). از پشت اوی is a prepositional phrase 'from his rear/behind him' — اوی is the archaic/genitive form 'his', so the clause means 'when Homan and Nestīhen were (separated) from behind him.'
segments.s267-g108: جدا ماند شد is a compound: جدا (apart) + ماند (remained) with شد as an auxiliary/past marker — 'were left apart.' باد here is the optative/particle 'may (it be)' common in classical Persian poetry, not the literal 'wind' sense by itself; followed by در مشت اوی ('in his fist') giving the wished-for result 'may wind be in his fist' (an idiomatic blessing/wish).
segments.s267-g109: گر is the conditional 'if'. از نامداران ایران نبرد is a noun phrase 'a battle with/from the famed men of Iran' where از introduces the counterpart/opponent (classical usage), نامداران ایران = 'the renowned men of Iran' (genitive relationship). نبرد is the noun 'battle/struggle' forming the condition 'if (he) wants battle with Iran's champions.'
segments.s267-g110: بخواهد is 3rd-person present/subjunctive 'he wants/desires'. بفرما is an imperative/hortative 'send him on his way / let him go' (polite/command form used here as an instruction). وز is an archaic/poetic form functioning like از 'from/of', and آن refers back to 'that matter'; برمگرد is an imperative 'turn back / withdraw' (addressing the addressee). Together: 'If he wants to fight... let him go; from that, turn back.'
segments.s267-g111: چو is 'when/if'. پیران نبرد is 'the elders' battle' (the elders taking up arms). تو جوید is present/imperative sense 'you seek' or 'you go after it' (جوید = seek/search), and دلیر is an adjective 'brave' linked to the exhortation: 'When the elders fight, you too seek the fight — be brave.' Word order places the conditional clause first, then the addressed action.
segments.s267-g112: مکن is the prohibitive 'do not'. بددلی ('ill‑will, spite') is the noun object: 'do not bear ill will'. پیش او means 'in his presence / before him' (پیش = in the presence of). شو is the imperative 'become' and چو شیر is a simile 'like a lion' ('be like a lion'). So: 'Do not be spiteful before him; become like a lion.'
segments.s267-g41: ز is the poetic preposition 'by/from' indicating cause or means. نیک‌اختر is an epithet/noun 'auspicious star' (a celestial favorable sign). گردش هور و ماه is a coordinated noun phrase: گردش 'revolution/rotation' (here the celestial circuit) governing هور (light/sun) and ماه (moon). The whole phrase supplies a celestial/astrological ground or auspice for what follows.
segments.s267-g42: ز again marks cause/ground: 'by/through'. کوشِش (here written کوشش) + نمودن is a verbal construction meaning 'to make an effort, to attempt' (نمودن is the light verb attached to کوشش). بر آن رزمگاه is a prepositional phrase 'upon/on that battlefield' (بر = on/upon, آن = that, رزمگاه = battlefield) giving the target/place of the effort.
segments.s267-g43: مرا is the dative/experiencer 'to me' (here written as the clitic parts 'م' + 'را'); this establishes the speaker's viewpoint. این درست است is the matrix clause 'this is true/certain'. کز is the subordinating particle 'that/from which' introducing the clause that gives the content (what is certain). کارکرد here means 'performance, deed' and is the noun governed by the subordinator.
segments.s267-g44: تو پیروز باشی is the subordinate clause meaning 'that you be/may be victorious' — باشی is subjunctive/jussive here expressing the assertion about the future outcome. به‌روز نبرد is a temporal/locative phrase 'on the day of battle' (به‌روز = ‘on the day/of the day’, نبرد = battle) placed after the verb phrase to mark when/where the victory is expected.
segments.s267-g45: نبیره is 'scion/offspring' (a noble descendant). کجا is the interrogative 'where/where is there' used rhetorically. چون تو 'like you' is a simile comparing others to the addressee. دارد is the ordinary verb 'has/possesses' and here links the question to نیا; syntactically the clause asks about the existence of any forebear/lineage matching the scion. The overall effect is a rhetorical question praising the uniqueness of the addressee.
segments.s267-g46: به جنگ اندرون is a compound prepositional phrase 'into/within the war' (به = into/towards, جنگ = battle, اندرون = inside). باشدش is a fused form of باشد + ـش (the enclitic pronoun) meaning roughly 'it will be to him' or 'it will happen to him'. کیمیا is the predicate noun 'alchemy/elixir' used metaphorically (a prized, transformational thing). The clause says figuratively that the battle will be, for him, like an elixir.
segments.s267-g47: ز شیران 'from/of the lions' sets the group. چه زاید 'what is born' is the question 'what comes forth/what is produced'; زاید is the verb 'to beget/bring forth'. مگر introduces the exception 'except' and نره شیر 'male lion' is the expected exception: 'nothing is born of lions but a male lion' — a rhetorical way to say only a male lion (i.e. a true lion) springs from lions.
segments.s267-g48: چنان چون بود is a past-tense descriptive phrase 'such as he was' (چنان = such, چون = as, بود = was). نامدار و دلیر are predicative adjectives 'renowned and brave' that characterize the subject recalled by the previous rhetorical question; adjectives follow the copula in this older/poetic word order.
segments.s267-g217: A string of coordinated nouns introduced by repeated به used instrumentally/stylistically ("with/by"). سلیح is an archaic spelling of سلاح (weapon). The repeated به before groups gives rhythmic emphasis rather than changing meaning.
segments.s267-g218: Continuation of the instrumental list with به + nouns. پیلان is the plural of پیل (elephants) and جنگی is a postpositive adjective meaning "warlike/for war," so پیلان جنگی = "war‑elephants." نر follows شیران as an adjective meaning "male," placed after the noun in typical Persian order.
segments.s267-g219: A protasis introduced by اگر "if." یار here means "ally/allying power," and باشد is the subjunctive/present form of بودن used in classical narration (rather than spoken است); جهان‌آفرین is the epithet for the Creator/God and is placed after باشد in inversion but reads as "if the Creator is (our) ally."
segments.s267-g220: نپیچیم is the 1st‑person‑plural negative form (ن‑ prefix + پیچیم) meaning "we will not/let us not turn/loosen." از ... عنان is a prepositional phrase literally "from here the reins" (عنان = reins/bridle), and تا به چین means "as far as/to China;" together the clause says we will not loosen/let go of the reins from here to China (i.e. over a great distance).
segments.s267-g221: چو بنشست is a temporal clause "when he sat down." فرزانگان را uses the object marker را after the definite noun "the sages," and بخواند is the past tense here meaning "he called/summoned" (classical past narrative form). Word order places the temporal clause first, then the object and verb.
segments.s267-g222: ابا expresses reluctance/objection and here provides a circumstantial nuance ("though reluctant" or "reluctantly"). نامداران = "the renowned/illustrious ones," and به رامش (به + رامش) means "into/into a state of calm/tranquility." نشاند is an archaic past causative/putative verb meaning "he seated/caused to sit," so the clause means he caused the famed men to be seated in calm despite reluctance.
segments.s267-g223: همی is a classical continuous/progressive particle (marks iterative or ongoing action). خورد is used idiomatically with دل ("the heart ate/was filled") meaning "the heart rejoiced/had pleasure." شادی‌کنان is an adverbial participle "joyfully," and به جای literally "in place" (i.e. contentedly), completing the idiomatic image.
segments.s267-g224: Another clause with همی marking continued action: با یلان "with the heroes." جنگ را is the definite object "the war/battle" (را marks it), and کرد رای is a compound where کرد (did/made) takes رای (plan/counsel) as object — "he made a plan/counsel (for war)."
segments.s267-g17: نخست (fronted adverb) marks this as the first point. آنک گفتی که is a literary complement clause marker: آنک = 'that/which' and گفتی is the 2nd-person past 'you said' introducing the content clause (که). مر is an enclitic/old form meaning 'to me' (indirect object) placed before the following definite object گیو را ('Giv' + object marker را).
segments.s267-g18: بزرگان فرزانه is a nominal phrase 'great/elder wise ones' (adjective after the plural noun in literary style). The coordinating و links another noun نیو; را placed after the coordinated phrase marks the whole coordination as a definite object/topic. نیو here functions as a proper name (poetic/old orthography).
segments.s267-g19: به نزدیک پیران is a prepositional phrase 'to/into the presence of the elders' (به + نزدیک = 'to/near to'); فرستاده‌ام is the perfect/compound past first person 'I have sent' (agent = I). The direct object ('him') is omitted here because it was named earlier (Giv).
segments.s267-g20: چه مایه is an exclamatory/quantifying phrase 'what an amount' or 'so much'; ورا ('to him') is the enclitic dative pronoun referring back to the person sent; پندها is the plural 'advices/admonitions'; داده‌ام is 'I have given' (first person perfect). Together: 'and I have given him so many counsels.'
segments.s267-g21: نپذرفت is the past tense negative 'he did not accept/refused'. از آن پس is a temporal phrase 'after that' (از + demonstrative آن + پس). خود او is emphatic 'he himself'. پند من is a genitive construction 'my advice' (noun followed by possessor).
segments.s267-g22: نجست is the past form of جست with a negative prefix ن- here meaning 'did not seek' (literary negated past). اندر این کار = 'in/within this affair'; پیوند من = 'my bond/connection' or 'my alliance' (noun + possessor). The clause means he did not consult/seek my help regarding this matter of my alliance.
segments.s267-g23: سپهبد (a military title) is the subject. یکی here is an indefinite 'a certain/one' used idiomatically with داستان زد ('told a story'); زد in epic usage can mean 'uttered/related' (literary use of زدن for performing/sounding). بر این adds 'about this' or 'on this matter.'
segments.s267-g24: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. دستور پیشین = 'the earlier/previous order'. برآورد is a past tense verb here 'he fulfilled/carried out' (brought to pass). کین is the object/result 'vengeance/hatred' — the line says that when the earlier command was executed, vengeance was produced/brought about.
segments.s267-g25: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause here ('that'/'who'). هر مهتری places the universal quantifier هر before the noun مهتری ('every great one/chief'). کو is an archaic connective here (classical Persian often uses کو as a relative 'who' or 'whose'), and روان کاستست is a compound: روان 'soul' + کاستست, an archaic past-form meaning 'was taken away/was diminished' (i.e. he died or his spirit was removed). Word order is typical Classical Persian: quantifier + head + relative, then comment about the head.
segments.s267-g26: ز نیکی is a short, poetic prepositional phrase ('from/though/despite goodness') using archaic ز = 'from/because of'. به بخت بد is another prepositional phrase 'by/with ill-fortune' (بخت بد = bad fate). آراستست is an archaic perfect/past-form meaning 'was adorned/was set upon' (i.e. 'met with' or 'suffered'); together the phrase says that such a person is 'arrayed/visited by ill fate' despite his goodness. Note the reversed order of causes and result typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s267-g27: مرا زان سخن پیش بود آگهی is a fixed classical construction: مرا (to me) + زان سخن ('from that speech') + پیش بود ('previously was') + آگهی ('knowledge/information'). Literally 'to me from that speech beforehand was knowledge' = 'I had prior knowledge of that speech.' (Note: the tokenizer split مرا into م + را; here those two together function as the dative/experiencer 'to me'.)
segments.s267-g28: که introduces the content of that knowledge ('that ...'). پیران دل is a genitival phrase 'the elders' hearts' (pīrān as possessor of دِل). از کین نخواهد تهی uses از کین 'of/from hatred' and نخواهد (classical modal/auxiliary) + تهی 'empty' to mean 'will not be empty' i.e. 'will not be free of enmity.' The verb نخواهد expresses future/intentual sense in classical lines.
segments.s267-g29: ولیکن is a formal conjunction 'but/yet.' از آن links back to the previous remark ('from that'), and خوب کردار او is a noun phrase 'his good conduct' with the possessive pronoun او placed after the noun (classical Persian order). The whole segment sets up the contrast: 'but from that good deed/conduct of his ...'.
segments.s267-g30: نجستم is the 1st-person past of جستن ('I sought/attempted to find out'); in this context it carries the sense 'I could not ascertain' (the negative prefix ن‑ here conveys inability in usage). همی is an adverb with continuous/emphatic force ('ever/indeed'), and ژرف پیکار او is an NP 'the depth of his battle/struggle'. Together: 'I could not fathom/ascertain the profound depth of his fight.'
segments.s267-g31: کنون 'now' + آشکارا 'plainly/evidently' modifies نمود, an archaic/past verb meaning 'showed/revealed' (here 'has shown'). این سپهر 'this heaven/sky' is the agent/topic placed after the verb for emphasis (poetic word order); overall: 'Now the heavens have made this clear.'
segments.s267-g32: که introduces the clause being shown. پیران is the subject 'the elders'; به توران گراید is a verbal phrase with گراید (classical present/indicative of گراییدن) meaning 'turns/leans toward Turan' (به marks direction). به مهر is an adjunct of manner 'with affection' or 'in goodwill' (مهر = love/favour). The clause thus reads 'that the elders incline toward Turan out of affection.'
segments.s267-g113: به پیکار is a prepositional phrase 'to/into battle' (به + noun). مندیش is an archaic/poetic 2nd‑person directive meaning ‘be not doubtful/hesitant’ (here controlling the action); ز marks the opponent (poetic 'from/against') and names افراسیاب as the target. Word order places the destination/scene (به پیکار) up front, then the comment about attitude (مندیش), and finally the opponent.
segments.s267-g114: به جای introduces a contrast 'instead' (literally 'in place of'); آر here is a verb used imperatively/optatively 'put/arrange/replace' in an archaic/poetic register. دل روی are two coordinated nouns ('heart and face') that function as the things being redirected; از او marks 'from him' (source/away from the person), and برمتاب is the main verb of this clause (imperative/command form) meaning 'turn/avert' (i.e. turn away). The clause therefore issues the contrasting instruction to turn heart and face away from him.
segments.s267-g115: چو آید is a temporal clause 'when/if he comes' (چو = when, آید = he comes). به جنگ اندرون is a locative phrase 'into the interior of battle / into the fight' (به = to/into, جنگ = battle, اندرون = within). جنگجوی ('warrior') names the type of person the following statement concerns; the word order places the time/place before the subject.
segments.s267-g116: نباید که is an impersonal negative modal 'one must not / it should not be that…' (introducing an obligation/ prohibition). برتابی is the verbal idea 'endure/turn away/withdraw' (here the thing one must not do); از جنگ روی is a prepositional phrase 'turn the face away from the battle' (از = from, جنگ = battle, روی = face), with روی functioning as the thing turned. Overall: 'a warrior must not turn his face from the battle.'
segments.s267-g117: بر ایشان 'against them' uses بر in the sense 'against/over' followed by ایشان 'them'. تو پیروز باشی is an optative/subjunctive blessing/wish 'may you be victorious' (تو = you, پیروز باشی = may you be victorious). بجنگ is a bound adverbial/locative 'in battle' (literally 'in fighting'), so the whole segment expresses a wish for victory in battle.
segments.s267-g118: نگر is an imperative 'look/see' or 'consider'. دل نداری here states the issue about spirit/courage: دل (heart/courage) + نداری (lack) — literally 'you have/not have heart' but in context the speaker warns against lacking heart. بدین is a compound meaning 'with/regarding this' (here written as ب + دین in the tokenization); کار تنگ is a noun phrase 'a tight/pressing (difficult) affair'. Together: 'See — do not be lacking in heart regarding this pressing task.'
segments.s267-g119: چنین دارم اومید is a simple declarative 'thus I have hope' (چنین = thus/so, دارم = I have, اومید = hope). از کردگار is a prepositional phrase 'from the Creator/God' identifying the source of that hope. Word order is subject/verb/compliment typical of the narrative register.
segments.s267-g120: که introduces a content clause 'that…'. پیروز باشی تو is an optative/subjunctive phrase 'that you be victorious' (پیروز = victorious, باشی = subjunctive/optative 'may you be', تو = you). در کارزار is a locative 'in/on the battlefield' (در = in, کارزار = battle/field), giving the domain of the hoped‑for victory.
segments.s267-g177: که چون = a concessive/temporal clause head: 'that when'. Here چون functions as a temporal 'when/since' and the clause uses a poetic word order: prepositional phrase بر سپه 'upon the host' precedes the verb phrase گستریده است مهر, where گستریده است (perfect/passive participle + copula) means 'has been spread' and مهر is the patient/thing spread (here 'favour/kindness').
segments.s267-g178: چگونه = 'how' (introducing the manner); ز = 'from / because of'; پیغام = 'message'. بگشاد is a past/past‑participle form meaning 'opened/revealed' (here transitive/intransitive sense 'opening' of the face), and چهر (face) follows the verb in a poetic V–O order: 'how (did) the face open because of the message'.
segments.s267-g179: پس = 'then' (temporal/consecutive). آن نامه = 'that letter' (demonstrative + noun). شهریار جهان is a genitive/title phrase 'the sovereign/king of the world' modifying نامه: 'the king‑of‑the‑world's letter'.
segments.s267-g180: به گودرز داد = 'gave to Goudarz' (به + indirect object + داد 'gave'); و = 'and'; درود مهان is a brief poetic phrase 'greetings (درود) of/for the great/guests' — here functioning as an accompaniment to the act of giving (i.e. he delivered the letter and offered greetings).
segments.s267-g181: نوازیدن here is a deverbal noun/infinitive meaning 'flattery' or 'caressing/soft words'; شاه بشنید = 'the king heard' (subject + past verb بشنید); ازوی = 'from him' specifies the source: 'the king heard the flattery from him.'
segments.s267-g182: بمالید (past) = 'rub/press/anoint' (here a gesture of honour). بر نامه‌بر = 'upon/on the letter‑carrier' (بر + نامه‌بر); چشم و روی = '(to his) eyes and face' — the phrase denotes the physical honouring gesture (touching/pressing the messenger to one's eyes/face). Note the verb precedes the prepositional object in this poetic order.
segments.s267-g183: چو = 'when'. بگشاد (past) = 'opened/revealed' and مهرش = 'his favour/affection' (مهر with possessive -ش). به خواننده داد = 'he gave to the reciter' (به + indirect object + داد); the clause sets up that, upon revealing his favour, he bestowed something on the reciter.
segments.s267-g184: سخنها = 'the words' (direct object). بر او کرد خواننده یاد is a poetic inversion: کرد (to make/do) + خواننده (the reciter) + یاد (remembrance) + بر او ('upon him'). Read naturally: 'the reciter made the words a remembrance to him' → 'the reciter reminded him of those words.' Note the unusual word order places the agent (خواننده) after the verb for metrical/poetic reasons.
segments.s267-g49: Word order is inverted from a neutral Modern Persian order; the clause reads literally 'this deed of yours is not upon injustice.' به + noun phrase (به بیداد) marks the semantic domain ('with/in regard to injustice'), بر is the prepositional element 'on/upon,' and نیست is the copula negation 'is not.' این کار تو is the nominal phrase 'this deed of yours' and serves as the topic/subject of the negative statement.
segments.s267-g50: بسنده است is a predicative phrase 'is sufficient'; the verb است follows the predicate (inversion common in poetry). یزدان نگهدار تو is a noun phrase 'God, your guardian' where the possessive pronoun تو follows the noun (classical order). The whole means 'God the guardian of yours is sufficient.'
segments.s267-g51: This is a nominative/topic fragment: تو (you) marks the addressee, followed by the coordinated nouns زور و دلیری ('strength and bravery'). ز introduces the source ('from') and یزدان‌شناس is the compound 'God‑knower' / pious person indicating the source. The main verb is deferred to the next segment (ellipsis of the verb).
segments.s267-g52: از او دار is an imperative/hortative instruction 'take/have from him' where دار functions as an archaic or poetic imperative of 'داشتن/گرفتن' (here treated as 'take/receive'); because of the provided sense list mismatch the exact dictionary sense is not available. تا + subjunctive (زنده باشی) introduces a temporal clause 'as long as you live' (باشی is subjunctive/optative here), and سپاس is the noun 'thanks' (object or exhortation to be thankful).
segments.s267-g53: سه دیگر ('three others') is the topical noun phrase; the first که is the relative/complementizer 'that' linking to گفتی ('you said'), and the second که begins the reported clause 'that Afrasiab ...'. Note that گفتی here is the reported-speech verb 'you said' (the provided sense list does not match that verbal use).
segments.s267-g54: سپه را is the definite object with the narrow-focus marker را ('the army'); همی is a classical habitual/continuous marker often used before verbal forms; بگذارند is a 3rd-person plural present/subjunctive/optative 'they will let/they would cause' (here meaning 'make/let [the army] depart'); ز + آب means 'from the water' (indicating removal or departure from the water).
segments.s267-g55: ز پیران فرستاده شد is a passive construction 'was sent by the elders' (فرستاده as past participle/adjective 'sent' plus auxiliary شد). نزد اوی is a prepositional phrase 'to his presence / to him,' with اوی an archaic third‑person pronoun form. Word order and auxiliaries reflect classical Persian passive formation.
segments.s267-g56: سپاهش is the noun سپاه plus the possessive clitic -ش 'his' = 'his army.' نهادست is an archaic compound verb (= نهاد + است) meaning 'is placed/has been set' and is followed by روی ('face') used figuratively; به ایران indicates the target/direction 'toward Iran.' Together the phrase means 'his army set its face toward Iran / turned toward Iran.'
segments.s267-g89: تو is the 2nd-person pronoun 'you'. بشناس is an imperative/hortative 2nd-person-singular meaning 'know' or 'be aware'; it introduces the subordinate locative clause beginning with کو ('where'). کو is the interrogative/relative 'where' (poetic), and شهر آباد خویش is the noun phrase ('your prosperous city') with آباد modifying شهر and the possessive expressed by خوی + ش (see next segment). Word order places the verb 'بشناس' before the clause it governs.
segments.s267-g90: بر و بوم is a coordinated pair meaning 'domain / homeland' (two nouns joined by و). فرخنده بنیاد خویش is a noun phrase 'his/your auspicious foundation' where فرخنده ('auspicious') modifies بنیاد ('foundation') and the possessive is indicated by خوی + ش (the reflexive/possessive 'own' formed by خوی + enclitic ش). The enclitic ش attaches to the phrase and yields 'خویش' = 'one's own'; the whole phrase continues the complement of 'کو' from the previous segment.
segments.s267-g91: به گفتار پیران is a prepositional phrase meaning 'according to the words/counsel of the elders' (به = 'according to/to', گفتار = 'speech/counsel', پیران = 'elders'). نماند is a negative present/predictive form 'does not remain / will not remain' and combines with بجای ('in place' / 'as it should be') to mean 'does not remain in its place / does not persist as the elders said.' The object of this clause is implicit (what follows in the next clause).
segments.s267-g92: This clause has two verbs describing the same subject: سپارد 'delivers/entrusts' and نهد 'places/sets'. The phrase به دشمن ('to the enemy') is the indirect object/goal, سپه (the thing delivered) is implicit from context, and پیش پای ('before the foot', i.e. 'at their feet') is a prepositional/complement phrase showing humiliating submission. The sequence means 'he will hand it over to the enemy and place it at their feet.'
segments.s267-g93: نجنباند is a negative past/present literary form 'did not move / did not stir' (3rd-person). او is the subject 'he'. داستان را uses the object marker را to mark 'the tale' as definite object. دو لب ('two lips') is an instrumental/expressive phrase indicating 'with his two lips' (i.e. by uttering); syntactically the instrument follows the object in this poetic order, so the sense is 'he did not utter the tale with his lips.'
segments.s267-g94: که introduces a result/restrictive clause 'so that / so that it was the case that'. ناید here is used in the sense 'does not come / did not come' (classical usage). خبر زو به من is the phrase 'news from him to me' (ز = 'from' + the clitic pronoun forming 'from him'); روز و شب means 'day and night' (an adverbial). Altogether: 'so that no news came from him to me day and night.'
segments.s267-g95: بدان روز functions as a temporal phrase 'on that day' (بدان = 'in/on that' in poetic diction, روز = 'day'). هرگز مبادا is a fixed, emphatic optative/negative formula meaning 'may it never be / God forbid.' درود ('greeting' or 'salutation') is the noun that is negated by the formula: 'may there never be greeting on that day.'
segments.s267-g96: که introduces the explanatory/cause clause 'for / because'. او is 'he'. بگذراند is the 3rd-person verb 'causes to pass / makes cross' (of the army). سپه را marks 'the army' as the definite object with را. ز رود ('from/over the river') uses the poetic preposition ز = 'from / across' and رود = 'river'. The clause therefore explains the reason for the curse: 'for he will cause the army to cross the river.'
segments.s267-g73: بدان‌ای is an imperative verb (بدان = 'know this') combined with the vocative ای addressing the addressee; سپهدار is the vocative 'O commander'. و links the two predicates. آگاه باش is a second imperative: آگاه (adjective 'aware') + باش (imperative 'be'), so the clause reads 'Know this, O commander, and be aware.'
segments.s267-g74: به هر کار is an adverbial phrase ('in/for every affair') with به marking the domain and هر کار = 'every undertaking'. با بخت همراه باش is the main imperative: با بخت ('with fortune') + همراه (predicative 'accompanied') + باش (imperative 'be'), literally 'be accompanied by fortune.' Word order is typical of older/poetic Persian where the predicate follows its object-like phrase.
segments.s267-g75: کزان سو is a locative phrase ('from that side') with کزان = 'from which' + سو = 'side/direction'. که introduces a relative clause. شد is the past verb 'became', and رستم شیرمرد is a nominal clause with رستم as subject and شیرمرد as predicate nominative ('Rostam became a champion').
segments.s267-g76: ز کشمیر و کابل is a poetic prepositional phrase 'from Kashmir and Kabul'. برآورد گرد is a compact/poetic verbal phrase: برآورد (bring forth / caused to issue) with گرد functioning as an auxiliary/verb of movement/coming-about, producing the sense 'came forth/issued out'. The phrase therefore reports something issuing forth from those regions.
segments.s267-g77: وز here functions as an archaic connective meaning roughly 'and from' (a contraction of و + از); آن‌سو = 'that side/over there'. که introduces the relative clause. شد is the past 'became'. اشکش is a proper name here (Ashkash) and تیزهوش is an epithet 'quick‑witted' modifying that name.
segments.s267-g78: برآمد is a past verb 'arose/issued forth'. ز خوارزم marks the source 'from Khwarezm'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once', and خروش is the noun 'cry/uproar' that is the semantic subject of برآمد (poetic inversion: verb before subject).
segments.s267-g79: به رزم اندرون شیده is an adverbial cluster: به رزم ('toward/for battle') + اندرون ('inward/within') with شیده ('mad/crazed') functioning as a predicative adjective describing the subject's state. برگشت is the past verb 'he turned/returned', and از اوی is a prepositional phrase 'away/from him' using the archaic pronoun اوی.
segments.s267-g80: سوی شهر گرگان is the directional phrase 'toward the city of Gorgan'. نهادست is an archaic/perfect-form verb 'he set/placed' (نهاد + ست), and روی ('face') is the object: the idiom نهادنِ روی = 'to set one's face toward', i.e. 'to turn/face toward'.
segments.s267-g57: همان است یکسر که گفتی سخن is a compact clause meaning 'it is exactly so, as you said.' همان است یکسر is a demonstrative + copula + intensive adverb (یکسر = 'entirely/utterly'), and که introduces the relative clause 'that which you said.' گفتی here functions as the clause's verb 'you said' (classical word-order places the relative-linker که before the quoted clause).
segments.s267-g58: کنون باز پاسخ فگندیم بن: کنون باز are adverbs 'now again'; پاسخ is the direct object 'an answer'; فگندیم is past first-person-plural ('we cast/threw/sent') — an archaic form of فکندیم. بن is a short poetic/archaic particle here (not the modern noun 'root') and functions as a locative/targetive particle in the line, so the whole phrase means 'now again we hurled/sent back an answer (at him).'
segments.s267-g59: بدان‌ای پر اندیشه سالار من begins with an imperative/attention marker: بدان‌ای = 'know this, O ...'. پر اندیشه is an adjectival phrase 'full of thought / thoughtful' modifying سالار (yearer/commander). سالار من is a possessive noun phrase 'my commander / my lord.' The vocative particle ای is fused to بدان to address the addressee directly.
segments.s267-g60: به هر کار شایسته کار من: به هر کار = 'in/for every affair'; شایسته modifies the following noun-phrase 'کار من' and means 'appropriate/worthy (to)'; thus شایسته کار من = 'worthy/appropriate to my work.' The repeating کار is a poetic reinforcement: 'every fitting action of mine.'
segments.s267-g61: که او بر لب رود جیحون درنگ: که introduces the clause; او is the subject 'he'; بر لب رود جیحون is a prepositional phrase 'on the brink/bank of the river Jeyhun' (لب = 'brink'); درنگ is the noun 'delay/pause' used here with the verb 'کرد' in the next segment (i.e. the construction درنگ کردن is split across the line).
segments.s267-g62: نه زان کرد کآید بر ما به جنگ: نه ... کرد is the negated past 'did not do that' (زان = 'that/from that'); کآید is a classical-present/subjunctive form meaning 'would come/come' (the prefixed کـ is an older form found in poetry); بر ما به جنگ = 'upon us in battle.' So the clause says he did not delay in order that he might come upon us in war.
segments.s267-g63: که خاقان بر او لشکر آرد ز چین: that introduces the clause; خاقان is the subject 'the Khagan / supreme ruler'; بر او لشکر آرد = 'brings/should bring an army against him' — آرد is the classical-present/subjunctive/optative form (classical narrative uses آرد where modern would use آورد or خواهد آورد); ز چین = 'from China' (ز is the poetic 'from').
segments.s267-g64: فراز آمدش از دو رویه کمین: فراز آمدش = 'from above came upon him' (فراز 'on high / from above'; آمدش = came to him, with the enclitic -ش = 'to him'); از دو رویه = 'from two sides' (رویه used poetically for 'side / direction'); کمین = 'an ambush/trap.' Word order places the source ('from above') before the verb for dramatic effect.
segments.s267-g121: همیدون is a proper name used vocatively here; گمانم is the 1st-person present of گمان + enclitic ـم, meaning “I suppose” or “I think.” که introduces the subordinate clause ('that'). چون is a temporal conjunction here meaning “when” (classical/poetic usage). ز راه is a short locative phrase “from/by the road” (ز = from, راه = road/way) specifying the manner/route of coming.
segments.s267-g122: به پشت سپاه is a prepositional/locative phrase: به + پشت = “toward/to the rear (of) the army,” where پشت = 'rear/behind' and the following سپاه is the noun modified. اندر آرم is a 1st-person singular present/optative-form of آردن/آوردن (classical spelling/poetic form) meaning “I will bring/put/set (in).” The final سپاه names what is brought — 'the (my) army/force' — so the whole clause means “I will bring (my) army to the rear of their army.”
segments.s267-g123: بر ایشان marks the target/against whom the action is done (‘against them’). شما is the (honorific/plural) addressee. رانده باشید is a past participle (رانده) plus the subjunctive/optative form باشید, a classical formula used to express a wish or blessing: “may you be having driven (them away)” → idiomatically “may you have driven them back.” کام (literally 'desire' or 'wish') stands as the next wished good—'may your desire be...' linking to the following segment.
segments.s267-g124: به خورشید تابان is a prepositional phrase 'to/unto the shining sun' (به = to/unto, خورشید تابان = the shining sun) and modifies the following predicate. برآورده here is a past-participial/adjectival use meaning 'brought about/raised/fulfilled' attached to نام ('name'), so the clause expresses the wish that the addressee’s name be elevated/realized 'to the shining sun.' In short, this is a continuation of the optative blessings: 'may your wish be fulfilled, [and] may your name be raised to the shining sun.'
segments.s267-g125: ز کاوس وز طوس is a classical compressed phrasing meaning 'from Kāvus and from Tūs' — ز = 'from' and the middle وز is an archaic/poetic sequence representing 'و + ز' (i.e. 'and from'). نزد سپاه means 'to/near the army' or 'in the presence of the army' (نزد here indicates the recipient/target). The whole phrase gives the source(s) and the recipient of the message.
segments.s267-g126: درود فراوان is a noun phrase 'abundant greetings' (درود = greeting(s), فراوان = plentiful). فرستاد is the simple past 'sent.' شاه is the subject following the verb (in Persian the order object–verb–subject is common in poetry/older prose), so 'the king sent abundant greetings.'
segments.s267-g127: بر آن نامه is the locative phrase 'on that letter' (بر = on/upon, آن نامه = that letter). بنهاد is the past tense of بنهادن (classical/poetic form of 'to place/put'), so 'Khosrow placed/put (something) on that letter.' خسرو is the agent/name 'Khosrow' and نگین (a signet or gem) is the thing placed: 'Khosrow placed a signet on that letter.'
segments.s267-g128: فرستاده را داد is 'gave it to the messenger' where فرستاده (messenger) with the object marker را is the indirect/direct object and داد is the past 'gave.' و کرد آفرین is a short clause meaning 'and he made/poured forth praise' — کرد (did/made) + آفرین (acclamation/praise) → 'and he praised (him)/and said “bravo.”' The sequence is three actions: placed the signet, gave it to the messenger, and praised.
